content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 30,
1994
Ronald Beals
Gannett Outdoor
1311 Terminal Street
West Sacramento,
CA
95691
Re:
Your Request For Confirmation
Of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
1—94-296
Dear Mr. Beals:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act.’! Because your questions are general
inquiries,
we are treating your letter as a reguest for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (3) .!
Your letter accurately suimuarizes my advice that the mass
mailing sender identification provision of the Political Reform
Act does not pertain to billboard advertisements.
The Act contains
no provisions requiring identification of the “sponsor” or
supporting committee of a candidate on a billboard advertisement.
In addition,
as long as the bonus or discount given to a
candidate or committee is provided as part of the standard
business policy of the commercial advertiser to its customers,
and
is not provided for political purposes, the bonus or discount is
not a contribution to the candidate or committee.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2!
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Ronald Beals
page
2
If you have any questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Colleen McGee
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

sections.question_synthetic: Do mass mailing sender identification requirements under the Political Reform Act apply to billboard advertisements, and do standard business discounts provided to candidates or committees constitute political contributions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The mass mailing sender identification provision does not pertain to billboard advertisements, and standard business discounts or bonuses provided as part of a commercial advertiser's normal business policy are not contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter confirming prior telephone guidance. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; content organized as confirmation letter. Requestor accurately summarized prior advice according to FPPC response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do mass mailing sender identification requirements under the Political Reform Act apply to billboard advertisements, and do standard business discounts provided to candidates or committees constitute political contributions?

CONCLUSION: The mass mailing sender identification provision does not pertain to billboard advertisements, and standard business discounts or bonuses provided as part of a commercial advertiser's normal business policy are not contributions.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission September 30, 1994 Ronald Beals Gannett Outdoor 1311 Terminal Street West Sacramento, CA 95691 Re: Your Request For Confirmation Of Telephone Advice Our File No. 1—94-296 Dear Mr. Beals: You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided to you concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.’! Because your questions are general inquiries, we are treating your letter as a reguest for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (3) .! Your letter accurately suimuarizes my advice that the mass mailing sender identification provision of the Political Reform Act does not pertain to billboard advertisements. The Act contains no provisions requiring identification of the “sponsor” or supporting committee of a candidate on a billboard advertisement. In addition, as long as the bonus or discount given to a candidate or committee is provided as part of the standard business policy of the commercial advertiser to its customers, and is not provided for political purposes, the bonus or discount is not a contribution to the candidate or committee. 1/ Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, q. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2! Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Ronald Beals page 2 If you have any questions, please contact me at (916) 322—5662. Sincerely, Colleen McGee Political Reform Consultant Technical Assistance Division
embedding.summary: FPPC confirms informal telephone advice regarding billboard advertisement identification requirements and whether business discounts to candidates constitute contributions under the Political Reform Act.
